---
title: "The Roman Road"
description: "Walking a stretch of road that has been in use for two thousand years is a strange and grounding experience."
author: "Sophia Jaffer"
date: "2025-01-10"
categories:
  - Roman Britain
  - Walking
---

## Watling Street

Watling Street was already old when the Romans paved it. The route followed a ridgeway used long before the legions arrived — the straight lines we associate with Roman roads were imposed on a landscape already threaded with older paths.

You can still walk it. Large stretches of the modern A5 follow the Roman line almost exactly, which means that walking the old road often involves walking alongside heavy goods vehicles, which somewhat punctures the historical atmosphere. But there are quiet stretches, particularly in the Midlands, where the road cuts through farmland and you can walk on the actual agger — the raised embankment on which the road was built.

## The surface underfoot

What you feel underfoot, on these stretches, is something remarkable: a slight but definite firmness, a compaction that is different from the surrounding ground. Archaeologists can date the road's construction precisely. But the compaction is the result of two thousand years of use — Roman soldiers, medieval merchants, drovers, coaches.

Geology laid down over millions of years is one kind of deep time. A road worn down by use over two thousand years is another — more human, more legible.

## What roads carry

A road is, among other things, an argument about where things are. Watling Street argued that London connected to the northwest, that the legionary fortress at Wroxeter mattered, that the island could be administered from a central point. These arguments outlasted the empire that made them.

The A5 is still busy. The argument, apparently, still holds.
